front: Play: opens with a Fury convincing the ghost of Tantalus to cause disorder between his grandsons; ends with Atreus revealing that the title character has eaten his own sons
Play: a guard says the best vengeance is with a sword; another character responds, 'Thou speakest of the end of punishment. But I the punishment itself desire'
Play: the title character kills Lycus for usurping the Theban throne, then goes insane and murders his wife Megara and their children while Amphitryon watches
Play: a messenger narrates an arrow going through a character's neck and a roof becoming wet with 'scattered brains'
Work: the dead emperor is sentenced to play dice forever with a bottomless cup in the underworld; Caligula appears and claims him as a slave; the only surviving Menippean satire
Work: Clotho reveals the spindles of Augurinus, Baba, and the title character to Mercury; Our Lady of Malaria prevents the protagonist from sweet-talking Hercules
Philosopher: illustrated the first, second, and third motions with a story in which Plato began to strike a slave but froze his arm in midair
work: On Anger (De Ira) and Other Philosophical Works

front: Philosopher: was the primary non-Christian inspiration for De Constantia by Justus Lipsius
work: On Anger (De Ira) and Other Philosophical Works

front: Work: one letter appreciates the author's asthma for making him feel on the verge of death; another recounts a visit to Scipio Africanus's villa; one rejects Zeno's syllogism against alcohol
Philosopher: according to Cassius Dio, Boudica's revolt in Britain was sparked by forced loans imposed by this Roman official
Philosopher: death scene involved offering 'imago vitae suae' and a libation to Jupiter the Liberator while being assisted into a bathtub; his wife Pompeia Paulina attempted suicide
Philosopher: Harry Sandbach dismissed this thinker as a 'spare-time amateur philosopher'; Thomas Macaulay said reading him was 'like dining on nothing but anchovy sauce'
Philosopher: early Christians admired fake letters between this thinker and Saint Paul
Play: unlike Euripides' version, the title character blames herself for the deaths of her and Jason's children
Play: ends with Theseus trying to reassemble the pieces of his son's corpse after a chariot attack
Play: Tiresias performs a lengthy augury; Jocasta rips open her own womb; Creon speaks with the ghost of King Laius
Play: often misattributed to Seneca but actually describes his death; the title character is exiled to Pandataria
Philosopher: Martin of Braga wrote On Anger and Formula for an Honest Life inspired by this thinker; those works, addressed to King Miro, are the primary evidence of his 6th-century impact
work: On Anger (De Ira) and Other Philosophical Works

front: Play: contains a passage about a 'second Argo' sailing past the ocean's known bounds, which Christopher Columbus interpreted as a prophecy of the New World
